unit.name: Vectorized derivatives for a neural-network layer
unit.description: Derives Jacobians and parameter gradients for activations, affine layers, and the score function, including delta reuse and shape conventions.
unit.summary: The lecture now derives the gradients for specific neural-network building blocks. For an elementwise activation h = f(z), the Jacobian is diagonal because each output coordinate depends only on the matching input coordinate, so the diagonal entries are f'(z_i) and off-diagonal entries are zero [ts=1644s][ts=1715s]. For affine computation z = Wx + b, the derivative with respect to x is W and the derivative with respect to b is the identity matrix [ts=1763s][ts=1809s]. Using the small network example, the score derivative is expanded with the chain rule as ds/db = ds/dh · dh/dz · dz/db [ts=1997s], which simplifies using a Hadamard product for elementwise multiplication [ts=2081s]. The instructor then names the shared upstream gradient/error signal δ so it can be reused across parameter derivatives instead of recomputed [ts=2206s], derives the weight gradient as an outer-product style combination of upstream gradient and local input [ts=2417s], and warns that practical implementations follow a shape convention so gradient tensors match the shapes of the parameters they update [ts=2594s][ts=2620s].

key_points.text: The Jacobian of an elementwise activation is diagonal, with f'(z_i) on the diagonal and zeros elsewhere.
For z = Wx + b, the derivative with respect to x is W.
For z = Wx + b, the derivative with respect to b is the identity matrix.
The score derivative with respect to the bias is decomposed as ds/db = ds/dh times dh/dz times dz/db.
The Hadamard product denotes elementwise multiplication in neural-network gradient formulas.
The shared upstream gradient delta should be computed once and reused across ds/db and ds/dW calculations.
The weight gradient is formed from the upstream gradient and the local input in an outer-product pattern.
The shape convention stores gradients in the same shapes as their corresponding parameter tensors.

unit_summary_for_generation: The lecture now derives the gradients for specific neural-network building blocks. For an elementwise activation h = f(z), the Jacobian is diagonal because each output coordinate depends only on the matching input coordinate, so the diagonal entries are f'(z_i) and off-diagonal entries are zero [ts=1644s][ts=1715s]. For affine computation z = Wx + b, the derivative with respect to x is W and the derivative with respect to b is the identity matrix [ts=1763s][ts=1809s]. Using the small network example, the score derivative is expanded with the chain rule as ds/db = ds/dh · dh/dz · dz/db [ts=1997s], which simplifies using a Hadamard product for elementwise multiplication [ts=2081s]. The instructor then names the shared upstream gradient/error signal δ so it can be reused across parameter derivatives instead of recomputed [ts=2206s], derives the weight gradient as an outer-product style combination of upstream gradient and local input [ts=2417s], and warns that practical implementations follow a shape convention so gradient tensors match the shapes of the parameters they update [ts=2594s][ts=2620s].

kp_catalog.name: Chain rule for composed functions
kp_catalog.description: Gradients of composed scalar or vector functions are computed by multiplying derivatives or Jacobians along the computation path.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because chain rule for composed functions formalizes how gradients of composed scalar or vector functions are computed by multiplying derivatives or Jacobians along the computation path, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Layer-wise Jacobians for activations and affine maps
kp_catalog.description: Elementwise activations produce diagonal Jacobians, while affine maps such as Wx+b yield simple derivatives with respect to inputs and biases.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because layer-wise Jacobians for activations and affine maps clarifies elementwise activations produce diagonal Jacobians, while affine maps such as Wx+b yield simple derivatives with respect to inputs and biases, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Upstream gradient reuse and outer-product parameter gradients
kp_catalog.description: Shared upstream error signals should be reused across parameter derivatives, and weight gradients often combine the upstream signal with the local input in an outer-product form.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because upstream gradient reuse and outer-product parameter gradients clarifies shared upstream error signals should be reused across parameter derivatives, and weight gradients often combine the upstream signal with the local input in an outer-product form, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Shape convention for gradients
kp_catalog.description: In practice, gradients are stored in the same tensor shapes as the parameters they update, even when raw Jacobian form would have a different layout.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because shape convention for gradients clarifies in practice, gradients are stored in the same tensor shapes as the parameters they update, even when raw Jacobian form would have a different layout, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
